schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, the one being the chief magistrate and the other the high priest, both concerned in these laws; the witnesses note God speaking to Moses and Aaron together, to give the laws of clean and unclean meats — the joining of the lawgiver and the priest, both having to do with these laws of holiness (God speaking to both, that these laws may be taught and kept: 'the LORD spake unto Moses and to Aaron, saying unto them'; the persons, 'Moses and... Aaron,' the one being the chief magistrate, and the other the high priest, both concerned in the laws of clean and unclean; that Moses, the lawgiver, was to deliver the law, and Aaron, the priest, to teach the difference between holy and unholy, clean and unclean; the meaning the commentators draw, that the magistrate and the minister are both to uphold the holiness of God's people, the one by authority, the other by teaching; that these laws of distinction were for the keeping of Israel as a holy and separate people; that God would have his people taught to discern between clean and unclean).

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses and to Aaron
saying unto them
the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron together, the magistrate and the priest, in the laws of clean and unclean
Moses to deliver the law and Aaron to teach the difference between holy and unholy; these laws keeping Israel a holy and separate people

schools.summary: Gill: to them only belong the following laws, and not unto the Gentiles, as Jarchi observes; these are the beasts which they might eat; the witnesses note God charging Israel which of all the beasts of the earth they might eat — the appointing of the clean beasts for food, a law of distinction for God's people (God marking out what his people may eat: 'These are the beasts which ye shall eat among all the beasts that are on the earth'; the persons, 'the children of Israel,' that to them only belong the following laws, and not unto the Gentiles, the law of distinction given to God's covenant people; the matter, 'these are the beasts which ye shall eat,' that God appointed which of all the beasts were clean and fit for food; the meaning the commentators draw, that God, by these laws, taught his people to distinguish, and to be a separate and holy nation, not eating as the Gentiles did; that the distinguishing of meats was a means of keeping Israel apart from the heathen and reminding them of holiness; the type, that the clean and unclean shadow the difference between the holy and the profane, and that God's people are to discern and choose the clean).

themes: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, These are the beasts which ye shall eat
among all the beasts that are on the earth
God appointing which of all the beasts Israel might eat, a law of distinction for his covenant people
keeping Israel separate and holy from the heathen; the clean and unclean shadowing the difference between the holy and the profane

schools.summary: Gill: whatsoever parteth the hoof, that is, whose hoof is parted and cloven quite through, and cheweth the cud, that beast they might eat; the witnesses note God appointing as clean every beast that both parts the hoof and chews the cud — the two marks of the clean beast, that must meet together (both the divided hoof and the chewed cud required: 'Whatsoever parteth the hoof, and is clovenfooted, and cheweth the cud, among the beasts, that shall ye eat'; the first mark, 'parteth the hoof, and is clovenfooted,' the hoof parted and cloven quite through; the second mark, 'cheweth the cud,' that chews and brings up the food again; that both marks must be found together, the one without the other not making the beast clean; the meaning the commentators draw, that these natural marks were chosen of God to teach moral and spiritual lessons: the dividing of the hoof shadows a distinct and separate walk, the conversation parted from the world; and the chewing of the cud, the meditating on and digesting of the word of God; that the true believer has both, a holy walk and an inward feeding on the word, the two meeting together; that profession and practice, the inward and the outward, must go together).

debates.question: What spiritual lessons do the two marks of the clean beast teach?

sides.position: The dividing of the hoof shadows a distinct and separate walk, the conversation parted from the world; the chewing of the cud, the meditating on and digesting of the word of God — both meeting in the true believer, a holy walk and an inward feeding on the word, profession and practice together

themes: Whatsoever parteth the hoof, and is clovenfooted, and cheweth the cud, among the beasts
that shall ye eat
the two marks of the clean beast, the parted hoof and the chewed cud, which must meet together
some seeing a distinct, separate walk (the divided hoof) and the digesting of the word (the cud); both in the true believer

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the camel, though it chews the cud, does not divide the hoof, and so is unclean; the witnesses note God declaring unclean those beasts that have but one of the two marks, as the camel, which chews the cud but divides not the hoof — the rule that one mark alone does not make clean, the camel an instance (the camel, having but one mark, unclean: 'these shall ye not eat of them that chew the cud, or of them that divide the hoof: as the camel, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean'; the rule, that a beast must have both marks; having one of these descriptive characters but not the other, it is unclean; the camel, 'cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof,' an instance of a beast with the inward mark but not the outward; the meaning the commentators draw, that the chewing of the cud without the parted hoof may shadow an inward profession or knowledge of the word without a holy and separate walk; that a man may seem to feed on the word, yet, wanting a distinct walk, be unclean, profession without practice availing nothing; that both the inward and the outward marks must meet, the one without the other not enough).

themes: these shall ye not eat... as the camel, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof
he is unclean unto you
the camel unclean, having one mark but not the other (it chews the cud but divides not the hoof)
some seeing the cud without the parted hoof shadow profession or knowledge of the word without a holy, separate walk; both marks must meet

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the coney, because he cheweth the cud but divideth not the hoof, is unclean; the witnesses note God declaring the coney unclean, because it chews the cud but divides not the hoof — another instance of a beast with but one mark, unclean (the coney, like the camel, having one mark only: 'the coney, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean unto you'; the coney, or rabbit, a small beast that cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; that, like the camel, it has the inward mark, the cud, but lacks the outward, the parted hoof, and so is unclean; the meaning before given, that the inward mark without the outward, a seeming feeding on the word without a holy and separate walk, is not enough to make clean; that a profession of knowledge or devotion, unaccompanied by a distinct walk, leaves a man unclean; that God requires both the inward and the outward together).

themes: And the coney, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof
he is unclean unto you
the coney unclean, having the inward mark (it chews the cud) but lacking the outward (it divides not the hoof)
the inward without the outward not enough; some seeing a profession without a holy, separate walk leaving a man unclean

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the hare, arnebeth, as Bochart and others take it, an animal of the kind here described, which chews the cud but divides not the hoof; the witnesses note God declaring the hare unclean, because it chews the cud but divides not the hoof — a third instance of the one-marked beast, unclean (the hare too having one mark only: 'the hare, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof; he is unclean unto you'; the hare, though he chews the cud, divideth not the hoof; that, like the camel and the coney, it has the inward mark but lacks the outward, and so is unclean; the lesson before given, that the inward seeming, the chewing of the cud, without the outward distinct walk, the parted hoof, leaves the beast unclean; that three such instances are given, camel, coney, hare, to press the rule that the inward mark alone does not make clean; that knowledge or profession of the word, without a holy walk, is not enough).

themes: And the hare, because he cheweth the cud, but divideth not the hoof
he is unclean unto you
the hare unclean, a third instance of the one-marked beast (it chews the cud but divides not the hoof)
three instances pressing the rule that the inward mark alone does not make clean; knowledge of the word without a holy walk not enough

schools.summary: Gill: the swine, though its hoofs are parted and cloven quite through, yet it cheweth not the cud, and so is unclean; the witnesses note God declaring the swine unclean, because, though it divides the hoof, it chews not the cud — the opposite instance, a beast with the outward mark but not the inward, unclean (the swine having the outward mark only: 'the swine, though he divide the hoof, and be clovenfooted, yet he cheweth not the cud; he is unclean to you'; the swine, 'divide the hoof, and be clovenfooted,' having the outward mark, the parted hoof; the lack, 'yet he cheweth not the cud,' wanting the inward mark, not chewing or digesting the food; that, opposite to the camel, coney, and hare, inward without outward, the swine has the outward without the inward, and so is also unclean; the meaning the commentators draw, that the swine may shadow those who have an outward show of a distinct walk but no inward feeding on the word, a form of separation without the digesting of the truth, hypocrisy in the other direction; that an outward profession or walk, without inward grace and feeding, is as unclean as the inward without the outward; that the swine, with its filthy nature, is a fit emblem of the unclean).

debates.question: What does the swine, with the outward mark but not the inward, shadow?

sides.position: Those who have an outward show of a distinct walk (the parted hoof) but no inward feeding on and digesting of the word (the cud) — a form of separation without inward grace, as unclean as the inward without the outward; the swine, with its filthy nature, a fit emblem of the unclean

themes: the swine, though he divide the hoof, and be clovenfooted, yet he cheweth not the cud
he is unclean to you
the swine unclean, having the outward mark (it divides the hoof) but not the inward (it chews not the cud)
some seeing an outward show of a distinct walk without inward feeding on the word, as unclean as the inward without the outward

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: of their flesh they were not to eat, and their carcase they were not to touch; they were unclean; the witnesses note God forbidding Israel to eat the flesh or touch the carcase of the unclean beasts, the camel, coney, hare, and swine — the double prohibition, against eating and even touching the unclean (they to keep wholly clear of the unclean: 'Of their flesh shall ye not eat, and their carcase shall ye not touch; they are unclean to you'; the forbidding of eating, 'of their flesh shall ye not eat,' that none of these unclean beasts was to be eaten; the forbidding of touching, 'their carcase shall ye not touch,' that they were not even to touch the dead body of the unclean, lest they be defiled; the reason, 'they are unclean to you,' that these were unclean, and to be wholly avoided; the great lesson the commentators draw, that God's people are to keep wholly clear of the unclean, not only not partaking but not so much as touching it, touch not the unclean thing; that holiness keeps at a distance from defilement, abstaining from all appearance of evil; that we are to be separate from all that is unclean).

themes: Of their flesh shall ye not eat, and their carcase shall ye not touch
they are unclean to you
God forbidding Israel to eat the flesh or even touch the carcase of the unclean beasts
God's people to keep wholly clear of the unclean ('touch not the unclean thing'), holiness keeping at a distance from defilement

schools.summary: Gill: these they might eat of all that are in the waters, in the sea or in rivers, pools, and ponds, meaning fish that have fins and scales; the witnesses note God appointing as clean every fish that has both fins and scales — the two marks of the clean fish, that must meet together (both fins and scales required: 'whatsoever hath fins and scales in the waters, in the seas, and in the rivers, them shall ye eat'; the marks, 'fins and scales,' that the clean fish must have both, the fins by which it moves and the scales that cover it; that, as with the beasts, two marks were required, the one without the other not enough; the meaning the commentators draw, that these natural marks teach spiritual lessons: the fins, by which the fish makes way and rises in the water, may shadow the believer's progress and rising heavenward; and the scales, the covering and defense, the armour of holiness and the distinction from the world; that the clean soul, like the clean fish, has both the means of progress and the covering of holiness; that God's people are to make their way upward and be clothed with defense against the world).

debates.question: What do the two marks of the clean fish, fins and scales, teach?

sides.position: The fins, by which the fish makes way and rises, may shadow the believer's progress and rising heavenward; the scales, the covering and defense, the armour of holiness and distinction from the world — the clean soul having both the means of progress and the covering of holiness

themes: whatsoever hath fins and scales in the waters, in the seas, and in the rivers
them shall ye eat
the two marks of the clean fish, fins and scales, which must meet together
some seeing the fins (progress heavenward) and the scales (the armour of holiness, distinction from the world); both in the clean soul

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: all that have not fins and scales in the seas and rivers, of all that move in the waters, shall be an abomination; the witnesses note God declaring an abomination all that move in the waters yet have not fins and scales — the unclean of the waters, lacking the marks of the clean (whatever wants the marks an abomination: 'all that have not fins nor scales... of all that move in the waters... they shall be an abomination unto you'; the lack, 'have not fins nor scales,' that these, such as eels, lampreys, and the like, want the marks of the clean; the sentence, 'they shall be an abomination unto you,' that they were to be utterly avoided, not eaten; the meaning before given, that, wanting the fins, progress, and the scales, the covering of holiness, they are unclean, shadowing those who neither rise heavenward nor are clothed with holiness; that what lacks the marks of the clean is an abomination, to be shunned; that God's people are to abhor the unclean).

themes: all that have not fins nor scales... of all that move in the waters
they shall be an abomination unto you
all water creatures without fins and scales an abomination, wanting the marks of the clean
shadowing those who neither rise heavenward nor are clothed with holiness; what lacks the marks of the clean to be shunned

schools.summary: Gill: this is repeated again and again, to deter from the eating of such fishes, that they might have them in abomination; the witnesses note God charging that the unclean fish be held an abomination, their flesh not eaten and their carcases abhorred — the repeated and emphatic forbidding of the unclean of the waters (the abomination pressed home: 'they shall be even an abomination unto you; ye shall not eat of their flesh, but ye shall have their carcases in abomination'; the repetition, that the word abomination is repeated again and again, to deter from the eating of such fishes; the double forbidding, 'ye shall not eat of their flesh, but ye shall have their carcases in abomination,' neither eating nor touching the dead; the meaning the commentators draw, that God presses the abhorrence of the unclean by repetition, that his people might learn to loathe what is unclean; that we are to hold the unclean in abomination, abhorring that which is evil; that the repeated charge marks the importance of keeping clear of defilement).

themes: They shall be even an abomination unto you; ye shall not eat of their flesh
but ye shall have their carcases in abomination
the unclean fish held an abomination, their flesh not eaten and their carcases abhorred
the abomination repeated to teach the people to loathe what is unclean, abhorring that which is evil

schools.summary: Gill: this is repeated that they might take particular notice of this law concerning the fish; the witnesses note God repeating that whatever in the waters has neither fins nor scales is an abomination — the summing and repeating of the law of the fish, that it be well marked (the rule laid down again for emphasis: 'Whatsoever hath no fins nor scales in the waters, that shall be an abomination unto you'; the repetition, that the rule is repeated that they might take particular notice of this law; the rule, 'no fins nor scales... an abomination,' that the one plain test, the two marks, settles all, whatever wants them being unclean; the meaning before given, that the marks of the clean, fins and scales, progress and holiness, distinguish the clean from the unclean, and that whatever lacks them is to be abhorred; that God would have his people clearly know and mark the difference, that they might choose the clean and shun the unclean; that the law of distinction is plain, that none might err).

themes: Whatsoever hath no fins nor scales in the waters
that shall be an abomination unto you
the rule of the fish repeated, that whatever has no fins nor scales is an abomination
the plain test of the two marks settling all; God would have his people know the difference, to choose the clean and shun the unclean

schools.summary: Gill: no description or sign is given of fowls, as of beasts and fishes, but the unclean are named one by one, beginning with the eagle, ossifrage, and ospray; the witnesses note God naming the birds Israel should hold in abomination, beginning with the eagle, ossifrage, and ospray — the listing of the unclean fowls, named one by one, no mark given (the unclean birds forbidden by name: 'these are they which ye shall have in abomination among the fowls... the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the ospray'; that, unlike the beasts and fishes, no description or sign is given of fowls, but the unclean are named one by one; the first named, 'the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the ospray,' birds of prey, ravenous and feeding on flesh and blood; the meaning the commentators draw, that the unclean birds are chiefly birds of prey and carrion, cruel, greedy, and ravenous, shadowing the cruel, rapacious, and unclean ways forbidden to God's people; that God's people are to be unlike these, not greedy of gain, not cruel, not feeding on what is unclean; that the clean are gentle and harmless, the unclean fierce and ravenous).

debates.question: What do the unclean birds (chiefly birds of prey) shadow?

sides.position: The cruel, rapacious, and unclean ways forbidden to God's people — the unclean birds being chiefly birds of prey and carrion, ravenous and feeding on flesh and blood; God's people to be gentle and harmless, not greedy of gain, not cruel, not feeding on what is unclean

themes: these are they which ye shall have in abomination among the fowls... the eagle, and the ossifrage, and the ospray
they shall not be eaten, they are an abomination
the unclean birds named one by one, beginning with the eagle, ossifrage, and ospray, birds of prey
chiefly birds of prey and carrion, shadowing the cruel, rapacious ways forbidden to God's people, who are to be gentle and harmless

schools.summary: Gill: the vulture and the kite after his kind, that is, the several sorts of them, are among the unclean fowls; the witnesses note God naming the vulture and the kite, with their several kinds, among the unclean birds — the continuing of the list of unclean fowls, birds of prey (the vulture and kite unclean: 'the vulture, and the kite after his kind'; the birds, 'the vulture, and the kite,' both birds of prey and carrion, like the eagle before; the phrase, 'after his kind,' that the several sorts and species of these were all included; the meaning before given, that these ravenous and carrion-feeding birds shadow the cruel, greedy, and unclean ways forbidden to God's people; that the vulture, feeding on the dead, and the kite, swift to seize its prey, are emblems of the rapacious and unclean; that God's people are to be unlike these, gentle and clean).

themes: And the vulture
and the kite after his kind
the vulture and the kite, with their several kinds, among the unclean birds of prey
the vulture feeding on the dead and the kite swift to seize its prey, emblems of the rapacious and unclean ways forbidden to God's people

schools.summary: Gill: every raven after his kind, the red raven, night raven, water raven, river raven, wood raven, and the like, all unclean; the witnesses note God naming every kind of raven among the unclean birds — the including of all the raven's sorts in the unclean list (every raven unclean: 'Every raven after his kind'; the bird, 'every raven... after his kind,' that the red raven, night raven, water raven, wood raven, and all the sorts, were unclean; that the raven, a bird that feeds on carrion and the dead, is among the unclean; the meaning before given, that the carrion-feeding raven shadows the unclean, that which feeds on death and corruption; that the raven, sent out of the ark and returning not, is an emblem of the unclean and restless; that God's people are to be clean, not feeding on corruption).

themes: Every raven
after his kind
every kind of raven among the unclean birds, feeding on carrion and the dead
the carrion-feeding raven shadowing the unclean, that which feeds on death and corruption; God's people to be clean

schools.summary: Gill: the owl, by the word here used, seems to be meant the ostrich, the great and little owls being after mentioned; with the night hawk, the cuckow, and the hawk after his kind; the witnesses note God naming the owl, the night hawk, the cuckow, and the hawk, with its kinds, among the unclean birds — the continuing of the list of unclean fowls (these too unclean: 'the owl, and the night hawk, and the cuckow, and the hawk after his kind'; the birds, 'the owl... the night hawk... the cuckow... the hawk,' birds of the night and of prey, with all the hawk's sorts; that these are birds that fly by night or seize their prey, unclean; the meaning before given, that the night birds and birds of prey shadow the unclean ways of darkness and rapine forbidden to God's people; that the owl and night hawk, loving darkness, may shadow those who love darkness rather than light, and the hawk, the cruel and rapacious; that God's people are children of light, not of the night, and gentle, not rapacious).

themes: And the owl, and the night hawk, and the cuckow
and the hawk after his kind
the owl, night hawk, cuckow, and hawk among the unclean birds of night and prey
the night birds shadowing those who love darkness rather than light, and the hawk the cruel and rapacious; God's people children of light

schools.summary: Gill: the little owl, and the cormorant, and the great owl; Ainsworth translates the words just the reverse, taking them for several sorts of these birds; the witnesses note God naming the little owl, the cormorant, and the great owl among the unclean birds — the further listing of night birds and water-fowl of prey (these too unclean: 'the little owl, and the cormorant, and the great owl'; the birds, 'the little owl... the cormorant... the great owl,' owls of the night and the cormorant, a greedy devourer of fish; the difficulty of the exact names, that Ainsworth translates the words just the reverse, yet all are unclean birds; the meaning before given, that the night-owls shadow those who love darkness, and the cormorant, a greedy and insatiable devourer, those given to greed and gluttony; that the cormorant, never satisfied, may shadow covetousness, which is never filled; that God's people are to be content, not greedy, and children of light).

themes: And the little owl, and the cormorant
and the great owl
the little owl, cormorant, and great owl among the unclean birds of night and greed
the night-owls shadowing those who love darkness, the cormorant covetousness which is never filled; God's people to be content and children of light

schools.summary: Gill: the swan is a bird well known, but it is a question whether it is intended by the word here used; with the pelican and the gier eagle, all unclean; the witnesses note God naming the swan, the pelican, and the gier eagle among the unclean birds — the further listing of unclean fowls, with some doubt of the exact names (these too unclean: 'the swan, and the pelican, and the gier eagle'; the birds, 'the swan... the pelican... the gier eagle,' water-birds and birds of prey, all reckoned unclean; the difficulty, that it is a question whether the swan is intended by the word here used, the exact identification being doubtful, yet the unclean kind is clear; the pelican, a bird of the wilderness and of solitude, and the gier eagle, a vulture-kind; the meaning before given, that these unclean birds, whatever their exact names, shadow the unclean ways forbidden to God's people; that the law's intent is plain, though some names are now uncertain, that the unclean be avoided; that God's people are to be clean and unlike these).

themes: And the swan, and the pelican
and the gier eagle
the swan, pelican, and gier eagle among the unclean birds (the exact names now doubtful, the unclean kind clear)
the law's intent plain that the unclean be avoided; these shadowing the unclean ways forbidden to God's people

schools.summary: Gill: the stork, a bird of passage, has its name from kindness, which it exercises both to its dam and to its young; with the heron, the lapwing, and the bat; the witnesses note God naming the stork, the heron with its kinds, the lapwing, and the bat, closing the list of unclean birds — the ending of the catalogue of unclean fowls (these too unclean: 'the stork, the heron after her kind, and the lapwing, and the bat'; the birds, 'the stork... the heron... the lapwing... the bat,' the last of the unclean fowls; the stork, a bird of passage, whose name is from kindness, which it exercises both to its dam and to its young, yet still reckoned unclean; the bat, a creature of the night and of darkness, fitly closing the unclean list; the meaning before given, that all these, whatever their qualities, were unclean by God's appointment, to be avoided; that even the stork, kind to its own, was unclean, the law's distinction resting on God's will, not on the creature's seeming virtues; that God's people are to follow his appointment in distinguishing clean and unclean).

themes: And the stork, the heron after her kind
and the lapwing, and the bat
the stork, heron, lapwing, and bat closing the list of unclean birds
even the stork (kind to its own) unclean, the distinction resting on God's will, not the creature's seeming virtues; God's people to follow his appointment

schools.summary: Gill: rather 'every creeping thing that flies,' for what are designed are not properly fowls, but, as the Jewish writers interpret, winged insects that creep; the witnesses note God declaring an abomination every creeping thing that flies and goes upon all four — the unclean flying insects, creeping things that fly (the winged creepers an abomination: 'All fowls that creep, going upon all four, shall be an abomination unto you'; the kind, 'fowls that creep,' better every creeping thing that flies, not proper birds but winged insects that creep, going upon all four; the sentence, 'shall be an abomination unto you,' that these were unclean, to be avoided; the meaning before given, that the creeping things, low and earth-bound, shadow the unclean and base ways forbidden to God's people; that the creeping insect, crawling upon the earth, may shadow those of an earthly, grovelling mind; that God's people are not to grovel but to mind heavenly things).

themes: All fowls that creep, going upon all four
shall be an abomination unto you
the flying creeping things (winged insects) an abomination, low and earth-bound
some seeing the creeping insect shadow those of an earthly, grovelling mind; God's people to mind heavenly things

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: these may ye eat of every flying creeping thing that goeth upon all four, which have legs above their feet, to leap withal upon the earth; the witnesses note God allowing as clean those flying creeping things that have legs above their feet to leap upon the earth — the exception of the leaping insects, clean above the rest (those which leap above the earth might be eaten: 'these may ye eat of every flying creeping thing... which have legs above their feet, to leap withal upon the earth'; the exception, 'these may ye eat,' that among the flying creepers some were clean and might be eaten; the mark, 'which have legs above their feet, to leap withal upon the earth,' that these had jointed legs by which they leaped, rising above the ground, not merely crawling; the meaning the commentators draw, that the leaping insect, which rises above the earth rather than grovelling on it, may shadow those who, though low in the world, rise above earthly things, mounting upward in their affections; that the believer, though in the world, is not earth-bound, but leaps above it toward heaven; that the mark of the clean is the rising above the earth).

debates.question: What do the leaping insects (clean, with legs to leap above the earth) shadow?

sides.position: Those who, though low in the world, rise above earthly things rather than grovelling on the earth — mounting upward in their affections; the believer, though in the world, not earth-bound but leaping above it toward heaven, the mark of the clean being the rising above the earth

themes: these may ye eat of every flying creeping thing... which have legs above their feet
to leap withal upon the earth
the exception of the leaping insects, with legs to leap above the earth, clean above the rest
some seeing those who, though low in the world, rise above earthly things, the mark of the clean being the rising above the earth

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the locust, arbeh, and the others named seem to be four sorts of locusts, all clean and edible; the witnesses note God naming the four clean leaping insects, the locust, bald locust, beetle, and grasshopper, with their kinds — the naming of the clean leaping creatures, which Israel might eat (these four leaping kinds clean: 'the locust after his kind, and the bald locust after his kind, and the beetle after his kind, and the grasshopper after his kind'; the four, 'the locust... the bald locust... the beetle... the grasshopper,' which seem to be no other than four sorts of locusts, all clean by reason of their leaping legs; that the locust, though a small and lowly creature, was clean and might be eaten, as John the Baptist ate locusts; the meaning before given, that the leaping creatures, rising above the earth, shadow those who mount above earthly things; that the lowliest, who yet rise heavenward, are clean in God's sight; that God reckons clean not by greatness but by the rising above the earth).

themes: the locust after his kind, and the bald locust after his kind, and the beetle after his kind
and the grasshopper after his kind
the four clean leaping kinds (locust, bald locust, beetle, grasshopper), clean by their leaping legs
the locust, though small, clean and edible (as John the Baptist ate); the lowliest who rise heavenward clean in God's sight

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: but all other flying creeping things, which have four feet and do not leap, shall be an abomination; the witnesses note God declaring an abomination all flying creeping things, except the leaping four, that have four feet and crawl — the closing of the law of the flying creepers, all but the leaping unclean (all but the leaping kinds an abomination: 'all other flying creeping things, which have four feet, shall be an abomination unto you'; the exception kept, 'all other... excepting the four sorts before mentioned,' the leaping ones; the rule, that all the rest, the crawling flying creepers, which have four feet and do not leap, were unclean; the meaning before given, that the crawling creepers, which grovel on the earth and do not rise, shadow the earthly and grovelling, the unclean; that only those which rise above the earth are clean, the rest an abomination; that God's people are to rise above the earthly, not to crawl in it).

themes: But all other flying creeping things, which have four feet
shall be an abomination unto you
all other flying creepers, the crawling ones with four feet, an abomination; only the leaping four excepted
the crawling creepers shadowing the earthly and grovelling, the unclean; only those which rise above the earth clean

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: for these ye shall be unclean: that is, defiled by contact with their dead bodies, unclean until the even; the witnesses note God declaring that whoever touches the carcase of the unclean creatures is unclean until evening — the defilement that comes by touching the dead unclean, and its limit (contact with the dead defiling, till evening: 'for these ye shall be unclean: whosoever toucheth the carcase of them shall be unclean until the even'; the defilement, 'whosoever toucheth the carcase... shall be unclean,' that even to touch the dead body of the unclean was to be defiled; the limit, 'until the even,' that the uncleanness lasted till evening, when, after washing, it ceased; the meaning the commentators draw, that the contagion of death and uncleanness, spreading by touch, shadows the defiling power of sin, which spreads and pollutes; that God's people are to keep from the touch of the unclean, lest they be defiled; that even a light contact with the unclean brings defilement that must be cleansed; that sin is contagious and defiling, to be kept far off).

themes: And for these ye shall be unclean: whosoever toucheth the carcase of them
shall be unclean until the even
whoever touches the carcase of the unclean defiled, unclean until evening
the contagion of death and uncleanness shadowing the defiling power of sin; God's people to keep from the touch of the unclean

schools.summary: Gill: whosoever beareth ought of the carcase, that carries them from one place to another, out of the camp, city, village, or house or field, shall wash his clothes; the witnesses note God appointing that whoever bears any of the carcase must wash his clothes and be unclean until evening — the greater defilement of bearing the dead, requiring washing (to carry the unclean defiling the more, needing cleansing: 'whosoever beareth ought of the carcase of them shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even'; the bearing, 'beareth ought of the carcase,' that to carry the dead body, more than merely touch it, brought a greater defilement; the washing, 'shall wash his clothes,' that he must wash, the defilement reaching his garments; the limit, 'be unclean until the even,' the uncleanness till evening; the meaning the commentators draw, that the more one has to do with the unclean, the greater the defilement, and the greater need of cleansing; that those who deal closely with sin and the unclean are the more defiled and must be washed; that the defilement of sin requires washing, cleansing by water, the word and the Spirit).

themes: And whosoever beareth ought of the carcase of them shall wash his clothes
and be unclean until the even
whoever bears the carcase, more than touching it, must wash his clothes and be unclean until evening
the more one has to do with the unclean, the greater the defilement and need of cleansing; the defilement of sin requiring washing

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the carcases of every beast which has not both the marks, the divided cloven hoof and the chewing of the cud, are unclean, and defile by touch; the witnesses note God declaring the carcases of every beast that has not both marks unclean, defiling everyone that touches them — the defilement from the carcases of the unclean beasts, the dead of the unclean (the dead of those wanting the marks defiling: 'the carcases of every beast which divideth the hoof, and is not clovenfooted, nor cheweth the cud, are unclean unto you: every one that toucheth them shall be unclean'; the beasts, 'which divideth the hoof... nor cheweth the cud,' those wanting one or both marks of the clean, unclean; that their dead bodies were unclean, and to touch them was to be defiled; the meaning before given, that the unclean, even in death, spread defilement, the contagion of uncleanness reaching by touch; that God's people are to keep from the dead of the unclean, lest they be defiled; that sin and uncleanness, even in their relics, defile those who touch them).

themes: The carcases of every beast which divideth the hoof, and is not clovenfooted, nor cheweth the cud, are unclean unto you
every one that toucheth them shall be unclean
the carcases of the beasts wanting the marks of the clean defile everyone that touches them
the unclean, even in death, spreading defilement; God's people to keep from the dead of the unclean

schools.summary: Gill: whatsoever goeth upon his paws, or the palms of his hands, meaning such creatures whose feet are not divided into two parts but into many, like the dog, cat, bear, and the like; the witnesses note God declaring unclean all beasts that go upon paws, defiling whoever touches their carcase until evening — the uncleanness of the paw-footed beasts and the defilement of their dead (the clawed and padded beasts unclean: 'whatsoever goeth upon his paws, among all manner of beasts that go on all four, those are unclean unto you: whoso toucheth their carcase shall be unclean until the even'; the beasts, 'goeth upon his paws,' those whose feet are not divided into two parts, but into many, like the dog, cat, bear, and such; that these, wanting the divided hoof, were unclean, and their carcase defiled by touch; the limit, 'until the even,' the uncleanness till evening; the meaning before given, that the paw-footed beasts, often beasts of prey, shadow the unclean and rapacious, and that their dead defiles by touch; that God's people are to keep from the unclean and its defilement; that the contagion of uncleanness spreads by touch).

themes: whatsoever goeth upon his paws, among all manner of beasts that go on all four, those are unclean unto you
whoso toucheth their carcase shall be unclean until the even
beasts going on paws (clawed, like the dog, cat, bear) unclean, defiling whoever touches their carcase
wanting the divided hoof, often beasts of prey shadowing the rapacious; the contagion of uncleanness spreading by touch

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: he that beareth the carcase of them shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even; the witnesses note God appointing that whoever bears the carcase of these unclean beasts must wash his clothes and be unclean until evening, for they are unclean — the greater defilement of bearing the dead unclean, requiring washing (to carry the unclean defiling the more, needing cleansing: 'he that beareth the carcase of them shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even: they are unclean unto you'; the bearing, 'beareth the carcase,' that to carry the dead body brought the greater defilement; the washing, 'shall wash his clothes,' that he must wash, the defilement reaching his garments; the limit, 'be unclean until the even,' the uncleanness till evening; the reason, 'they are unclean unto you,' that these were unclean and defiling; the meaning before given, that the more one deals with the unclean the greater the defilement and the greater need of washing; that those who handle sin and the unclean must be cleansed; that the defilement of sin requires washing).

themes: he that beareth the carcase of them shall wash his clothes, and be unclean until the even
they are unclean unto you
whoever bears the carcase of the unclean beasts must wash his clothes and be unclean until evening
the greater defilement of bearing the dead, requiring washing; those who handle sin and the unclean to be cleansed

schools.summary: Gill: these shall be unclean among the creeping things that creep upon the earth, as distinguished from those creeping things that fly, the weasel, the mouse, and the tortoise after his kind; the witnesses note God naming the weasel, the mouse, and the tortoise among the unclean creeping things of the earth — the listing of the unclean creepers that go upon the ground (these earth-creepers unclean: 'these also shall be unclean unto you among the creeping things that creep upon the earth; the weasel, and the mouse, and the tortoise after his kind'; the kind, 'creeping things that creep upon the earth,' distinguished from the flying creepers before, these go upon the ground; the first named, 'the weasel... the mouse... the tortoise,' small creatures of the earth, unclean; the meaning before given, that the creeping things, low and earth-bound, shadow the unclean and the earthly-minded, those who grovel in the dust; that God's people are not to be like these, creeping in the earth, but to mind things above; that the lowly and unclean creepers are emblems of base affections).

themes: These also shall be unclean unto you among the creeping things that creep upon the earth
the weasel, and the mouse, and the tortoise after his kind
the weasel, mouse, and tortoise among the unclean creeping things of the earth
the creeping things, low and earth-bound, shadowing the unclean and earthly-minded; God's people to mind things above

schools.summary: Gill: the ferret, whatever creature is here meant, has its name in Hebrew from the cry it makes; with the chameleon, the lizard, the snail, and the mole; the witnesses note God naming the ferret, the chameleon, the lizard, the snail, and the mole, completing the eight unclean creeping things — the closing of the list of the unclean earth-creepers (these too unclean: 'the ferret, and the chameleon, and the lizard, and the snail, and the mole'; the creatures, the last of the eight named unclean creepers; the difficulty of the exact names, that whatever creature is here meant, the unclean kind is clear; the mole, a creature of the dark earth, burrowing in the ground, fitly among the unclean; the meaning before given, that these low, creeping, often blind and grovelling creatures shadow the earthly and unclean, those who burrow in the dust and love the dark; that God's people are children of light, not burrowers in the dark earth; that the law's intent is plain, the unclean to be avoided).

themes: And the ferret, and the chameleon, and the lizard
and the snail, and the mole
the ferret, chameleon, lizard, snail, and mole completing the eight unclean creeping things
these low, grovelling creatures shadowing the earthly and unclean, those who burrow in the dust; God's people children of light

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: these are unclean among all that creep: whosoever doth touch them, when they be dead, shall be unclean until the even; the witnesses note God declaring the eight creepers unclean, and that to touch them dead defiles until evening — the summing of the unclean creepers and the defilement of their dead (these unclean, their dead defiling by touch: 'these are unclean to you among all that creep: whosoever doth touch them, when they be dead, shall be unclean until the even'; the sentence, 'these are unclean to you among all that creep,' that the eight named were unfit for food, and not to be touched, at least when dead; the defilement, 'whosoever doth touch them, when they be dead, shall be unclean until the even,' that to touch their carcase was to be defiled till evening; the meaning before given, that the contagion of the unclean, spreading by touch, shadows the defiling power of sin; that God's people are to keep from the touch of the unclean and its dead; that even a light contact with the unclean defiles, and must be cleansed).

themes: These are unclean to you among all that creep: whosoever doth touch them, when they be dead
shall be unclean until the even
the eight creepers unclean, their dead defiling whoever touches them until evening
the contagion of the unclean, spreading by touch, shadowing the defiling power of sin; God's people to keep from its touch

schools.summary: Gill: upon whatsoever any of the eight creeping things, when dead, doth fall, whether vessel of wood, raiment, skin, or sack, it shall be unclean, and must be put into water to be cleansed; the witnesses note God appointing that whatever the dead creeper falls on, of wood, cloth, skin, or sack, is unclean, and must be put into water and is unclean till evening, then cleansed — the defilement of vessels and stuff by the dead, and their cleansing by water (what the unclean touches defiled, and washed: 'whatsoever... wherein any work is done, it must be put into water, and it shall be unclean until the even; so it shall be cleansed'; the things, 'vessel of wood, or raiment, or skin, or sack,' the common stuff of use, defiled by the dead falling on it; the cleansing, 'put into water... and it shall be cleansed,' that such things were to be washed in water and so made clean; the meaning the commentators draw, that the defilement of sin reaches the things we use, and that cleansing is by water, the washing of the word and the Spirit; that what sin has defiled must be washed to be clean; that there is cleansing for the defiled, by water).

themes: whatsoever... wherein any work is done, it must be put into water, and it shall be unclean until the even
so it shall be cleansed
whatever the dead creeper falls on (wood, cloth, skin, sack) unclean, to be put into water and cleansed
the defilement of sin reaching the things we use, the cleansing by water, the washing of the word and the Spirit

schools.summary: Gill: every earthen vessel, whereinto any of the above reptiles falleth, whatsoever is in it shall be unclean, and the vessel itself must be broken; the witnesses note God appointing that an earthen vessel into which the dead creeper falls, with all in it, is unclean, and the vessel is to be broken — the breaking of the defiled earthen vessel, which could not be cleansed but must be destroyed (the earthen vessel, defiled, broken: 'every earthen vessel, whereinto any of them falleth, whatsoever is in it shall be unclean; and ye shall break it'; the vessel, 'every earthen vessel,' that, unlike the wood, cloth, and skin which were washed, the earthen vessel was not cleansed but broken; the contents, 'whatsoever is in it shall be unclean,' that all in it was defiled; the breaking, 'ye shall break it,' that the porous earthen vessel, having drunk in the defilement, could not be washed clean, but must be destroyed; the meaning the commentators draw, that the earthen vessel, which cannot be cleansed but must be broken, may shadow the carnal heart, the old man, which sin has so soaked that it cannot be reformed but must be broken and made new; that some defilement is so deep that only the breaking of the old will do, a new creation needed; that the heart of stone must be taken away and a new heart given).

debates.question: What does the defiled earthen vessel, which must be broken (not washed), shadow?

sides.position: The carnal heart, the old man, which sin has so soaked that it cannot be reformed or cleansed but must be broken and made new — the porous earthen vessel having drunk in the defilement; the heart of stone taken away and a new heart given, a new creation needed

themes: every earthen vessel, whereinto any of them falleth, whatsoever is in it shall be unclean
and ye shall break it
the earthen vessel into which the dead falls unclean, and to be broken, not washed
some seeing the carnal heart, the old man, which sin has so soaked it cannot be reformed but must be broken and made new

schools.summary: Gill: of all meat which may be eaten, otherwise lawful and fit for food, that on which such water comes shall be unclean, and all drink in every such vessel; the witnesses note God appointing that food on which the defiled water comes, and drink in the defiled vessel, are unclean — the spreading of the defilement to food and drink (the unclean water and vessel defiling the food and drink: 'of all meat which may be eaten, that on which such water cometh shall be unclean: and all drink that may be drunk in every such vessel shall be unclean'; the food, 'all meat which may be eaten,' otherwise lawful and clean, yet defiled if the unclean water came on it; the drink, 'all drink... in every such vessel,' that drink in the defiled vessel was unclean; the meaning the commentators draw, that defilement spreads, the unclean touching and polluting even what was clean and lawful; that sin, like a defiling water, spreads to all it touches, making the clean unclean; that God's people are to keep their food and drink, their daily use, from defilement; that a little uncleanness pollutes much).

themes: Of all meat which may be eaten, that on which such water cometh shall be unclean
and all drink that may be drunk in every such vessel shall be unclean
food on which the defiled water comes, and drink in the defiled vessel, unclean, though otherwise lawful
defilement spreads, the unclean polluting even the clean and lawful; a little uncleanness pollutes much

schools.summary: Gill: everything whereupon any part of their carcase falleth shall be unclean, whether oven or ranges for pots, which, being of earth, must be broken down; the witnesses note God appointing that whatever any part of the dead creeper falls on is unclean, even the oven and the ranges for pots, which must be broken down, for they are unclean — the breaking down of the defiled oven, the earthen things destroyed (even the oven, defiled, broken: 'every thing whereupon any part of their carcase falleth shall be unclean; whether it be oven, or ranges for pots, they shall be broken down'; the things, 'oven, or ranges for pots,' the cooking-places, made of earth; the breaking, 'they shall be broken down,' that, being of earth, like the earthen vessel before, they could not be cleansed but must be broken down; the reason, 'for they are unclean,' wholly defiled by the dead; the meaning before given, that the earthen things which cannot be cleansed but must be broken shadow the carnal nature that must be destroyed, not reformed; that what sin has so defiled must be wholly broken down; that there is no patching the old, but a breaking down and a new).

themes: every thing whereupon any part of their carcase falleth shall be unclean; whether it be oven, or ranges for pots, they shall be broken down
for they are unclean, and shall be unclean unto you
even the oven and ranges, defiled by the dead, unclean and to be broken down, being of earth
some seeing the carnal nature that must be destroyed, not reformed; no patching the old but a breaking down and a new

schools.summary: Gill: nevertheless a fountain or pit, wherein there is plenty of water, a collection of waters, shall be clean, though that which touches the carcase is unclean; the witnesses note God appointing that a fountain or pit of much water remains clean, though what touches the carcase is unclean — the exception of the living and abundant water, not defiled (the spring of plenty staying clean: 'a fountain or pit, wherein there is plenty of water, shall be clean: but that which toucheth their carcase shall be unclean'; the exception, 'a fountain or pit, wherein there is plenty of water, shall be clean,' that the living spring or the great collection of water was not defiled by the dead falling in, the abundance and the flowing keeping it clean; the limit, 'but that which toucheth their carcase shall be unclean,' that what immediately touched the carcase was unclean, though the spring itself stayed clean; the meaning the commentators draw, that the fountain of plenty, not defiled, may shadow the inexhaustible fountain of grace, the living water in Christ, which the world's defilement cannot pollute; that the spring of living water, abundant and flowing, cleanses rather than is defiled; that the fountain opened for sin and uncleanness is never itself defiled).

debates.question: What does the fountain of plenty, not defiled by the carcase, shadow?

sides.position: The inexhaustible fountain of grace, the living water in Christ, which the world's defilement cannot pollute — the spring of living water, abundant and flowing, cleansing rather than being defiled; the fountain opened for sin and uncleanness (Zechariah 13:1) never itself defiled

themes: a fountain or pit, wherein there is plenty of water, shall be clean
but that which toucheth their carcase shall be unclean
the fountain or pit of plenty of water clean, not defiled by the dead, its abundance keeping it clean
some seeing the inexhaustible fountain of grace, the living water in Christ, which the world's defilement cannot pollute

schools.summary: Gill: if any part of their carcase fall upon any sowing seed that is to be sown, that which is selected to be sown, it shall be clean; the witnesses note God appointing that dry seed kept for sowing, on which the carcase falls, remains clean — the exception of the dry seed, not defiled (the dry seed staying clean: 'if any part of their carcase fall upon any sowing seed which is to be sown, it shall be clean'; the seed, 'any sowing seed which is to be sown,' the dry seed kept for the sowing; the clean, 'it shall be clean,' that the carcase falling on the dry seed did not defile it; the reason given by the commentators, that the dry seed, with its hard outer husk, did not take in the defilement, and was to be sown, not eaten, so remaining clean; the meaning the commentators draw, that what is dry and not opened to the unclean is not defiled, while that which is moistened and opened is; that the heart kept dry, closed to the unclean, is not so easily defiled; that God provides that the seed of the harvest be not defiled).

themes: if any part of their carcase fall upon any sowing seed which is to be sown
it shall be clean
the dry sowing seed, on which the carcase falls, clean, its hard husk not taking in the defilement
some seeing what is dry and closed to the unclean not defiled (unlike the wetted seed); the heart kept closed not so easily defiled

schools.summary: Gill: but if any water be put upon the seed, either accidentally or on purpose, and any part of their carcase fall thereon, it shall be unclean; the witnesses note God appointing that seed which has had water put on it, if the carcase fall on it, is unclean — the defilement of the wetted seed, opened to the unclean (the moistened seed defiled: 'if any water be put upon the seed, and any part of their carcase fall thereon, it shall be unclean unto you'; the water, 'if any water be put upon the seed,' that the seed, once wetted, either accidentally or on purpose, was opened and softened; the defilement, 'it shall be unclean,' that the carcase falling on the wetted seed defiled it, the moisture having opened it to the defilement; the meaning the commentators draw, that, unlike the dry seed, the wetted seed, opened and softened, took in the defilement; that the heart opened and softened to the unclean is defiled by it, while the heart kept closed is not; that we are to keep ourselves from being opened to the touch of the unclean; that what receives and takes in the defilement is made unclean).

themes: if any water be put upon the seed, and any part of their carcase fall thereon
it shall be unclean unto you
the wetted seed, on which the carcase falls, unclean, opened and softened to take in the defilement
some seeing the heart opened to the unclean defiled by it, while the heart kept closed is not; what takes in defilement made unclean
